The Trillion-Dollar Solution
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Now that the flat tax has faded, at least momentarily, from the center stage  of politics, free-market ideologues have geared up for another crusade. This time their banners say, "Privatize Social Security!" 

     In its purest form -- as proposed, for example, by the libertarian Cato Institute -- privatizing Social Security would mean dismantling our 60-year-old  retirement insurance system and replacing it with mandatory individual retirement accounts.

WORLD NEWS BRIEFS;U.S. Official Dismissed; Opposed All Land Mines
nytimes_articles.abstract:
In a speech today at Georgetown University, Defense Secretary William J. Perry said the United States military relies on thousands of land mines to defend against attack from North Korea. That means it will be years before the United States abolishes them, he added. In the meantime, the Pentagon plans to use land mines that are designed to self-destruct after a certain period. 

     "The longer-term step is to eliminate antipersonnel land mines altogether," Mr. Perry said. But, he added, "I think it is going to take some time to achieve that objective."

Columbia Defeats the P.C. Separatists
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Some of my fellow students at Columbia staged a two-week protest this month to try to force the university to establish an ethnic studies department. Such a department would be wrong. Twenty-two students were arrested after more than 150 occupied Low Library, the main administrative building. About 100 blocked the central classroom building for four days to protest the school's "hegemonic and  white supremacist" curriculum. 

     While I admire such activism and dedication, these tactics only disrupted the education for which we pay so much. Many students disagreed, not only with the means used but with the ends demanded.

Here's a Stadium Plan That Will Work
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Faced with shrinking revenues and cuts in services, New York City has to find ways to grow. Done a little differently, Mayor Rudolph Giuliani's proposal to build a ball park over the Long Island Rail Road yards in the West 30's offers this chance -- without dislocating residents or businesses. 

     In fact, it's a mistake to cast such a proposal as a sports project. The goal should not be simply to create a stadium but to develop an engine of growth that can create jobs and revenues for years to come.

Once again, the Ebola virus has appeared in monkeys in the United States. This time, monkeys imported from the Philippines for medical research were found to be infected with Ebola-Reston, a strain that is not dangerous to humans, at a primate center in Alice, Tex. 

     In 1989, the same virus turned up in monkeys at a research laboratory in Reston, Va. After that scare, officials at the Centers for Disease Control promised that improved quarantine measures would protect the country from a recurrence.

wiki_events.headline: Four people are killed and 75 are injured in a train accident in Jokela, Finland.

pages.title: Jokela rail accident
pages.description: Train accident in Tuusula, Finland, on 21 April 1996
pages.abstract: The Jokela rail accident occurred on 21 April 1996, at 07:08 local time in Tuusula, Finland, approximately 50 kilometres (30 mi) north of Helsinki. Four people were killed and 75 injured when express train P82 from Oulu, bound for Helsinki, derailed in heavy fog. The overnight sleeper train was carrying 139 passengers and five crew members. The official investigation found the accident was caused by overspeeding through a slow-speed turnout.

pages.title: Jokela
pages.abstract: Jokela is one of the three administrative centers in the Finnish municipality Tuusula. It has a population of around 6,000 residents. The Jokela high school and the Jokela railway station serve the community. Many residents commute to the capital of Finland, Helsinki, which is about 50 kilometres (30 mi) away. The Jokela Prison, opened in 1993, is also located in the village.
